category_title: Students who make disruptive noises to gain teacher attention during independent work
minimal_context: Tom is supposed to be completing independent math work while the teacher is occupied with another student.
dialogue:
TUTOR: It is time for independent math work. Please work on your assignment while I help another student.

STUDENT: (Does not raise his hand) Hoo! (Makes a short, high-pitched noise)

TUTOR: I am busy working with another student right now.

STUDENT: (Forgets to raise his hand) Eeee! (Makes another high-pitched noise to get the teacher to come over)

TUTOR: Please continue your independent math work quietly.

STUDENT: (Makes a series of high-pitched noises that occur in short bursts, like a shriek, a hoot, and a yell)

options.A: Conduct careful pre-intervention functional assessments to identify relationships between the classroom environment and the student's behavior during independent math work.
options.B: Rely heavily on diagrams and equations displayed on paper as visual supports for the student's memory and processing during independent math work.
options.C: Provide praise statements more often than error correction statements during independent math work, maintaining a ratio of praise to behavior ranging from 3:1 to 10:1.
options.D: Tell the student what behavior is desired, when it should occur, and why it should occur (because it helps the student get what he wants).
